summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that a low-privilege OAuth client can create a webhook subscriber pointing to an internal HTTP listener on localhost, and that a subsequent model change triggers the server to POST serialized model data to that internal URL.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction pipeline should cause the Spree server to send an HTTP POST request containing serialized product data to an internal HTTP listener running on localhost:9999, demonstrating that the server makes requests to attacker-specified internal addresses.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built a Spree instance, created an OAuth application with write scope, created a webhook subscriber with url http://localhost:9999/ssrf, created a product to trigger the webhook, and the internal HTTP listener received the POST request with the webhook payload.
verification_method: A Ruby HTTP listener on port 9999 records all incoming requests. After creating the webhook subscriber and triggering a model change, the verify script checks whether the listener received a POST request to /ssrf with a JSON body and the X-Spree-Hmac-SHA256 signature header. The presence of this request confirms the server made an outbound connection to the internal address.
evidence_produced_by_run: The internal HTTP listener on localhost:9999 received a POST request to /ssrf after the webhook subscriber was created and a model change was triggered.
The request contained the X-Spree-Hmac-SHA256 header, confirming it originated from the Spree webhook dispatch system.
The request body was valid JSON containing the serialized product resource, confirming data exfiltration to internal addresses is possible.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
